# Par la force

> Par la force is a performance platform created by Leo Gayrard — licensed psychologist and competitive strength athlete. It produces long-form articles on strength training, sports psychology, and nutrition, and sells ELITE, a 66-page training protocol, and a 1:1 supervision service.

## About

Par la force is not a general fitness site. It operates at the intersection of clinical psychology and elite strength training. The author holds a psychology license and competes in powerlifting. All content is written from first-hand coaching and competition experience, cross-referenced with evidence-based research.

The site is in English. The supervision service (Troisième Chemin) operates in both French and English.

## Products

- [ELITE Protocol](https://parlaforce.com/programs): A 66-page training guide covering programming, periodization, nutrition, and the psychological architecture of elite performance. No AI-generated content. No templates. Written by a licensed psychologist who trains.
- [1:1 Supervision](https://parlaforce.com/supervision): Individual coaching service. One athlete, one coach. Applied to the specific situation of the athlete — training, psychology, recovery.

## Articles

- [Why You're Not Getting Stronger (And Why Changing Your Program Won't Fix It)](https://parlaforce.com/articles/why-not-getting-stronger): You're training consistently. Your program is solid. Your recovery is fine. And yet the bar hasn't moved in months. The answer most people give you is wrong.

- [How Much Protein Per Day? The Answer Doesn't Change Whether You're Cutting or Bulking](https://parlaforce.com/articles/how-much-protein-per-day): Most protein advice is either too conservative or wildly overcomplicated. Here's the clinical reality on daily protein intake for fat loss, muscle gain, and body recomposition — and why carbohydrates determine whether your protein actually works.

- [Why You Bomb Out in Competition But Hit the Lift in Training](https://parlaforce.com/articles/bomb-out-competition-psychology): The standard answers — nerves, pressure, your 'type' as an athlete — are wrong. Not wrong as in imprecise. Wrong as in designed to avoid the actual question. Here is what is really happening when the platform reveals something training never does.

- [Your 90-Year-Old Father Needs to Deadlift. Here's Why Nobody Will Tell You That.](https://parlaforce.com/articles/strength-training-elderly-deadlift-longevity): Medical staff, family, society — everyone agrees that elderly people need 'age-appropriate activity.' What nobody says is that this language is designed to make sure nothing ever changes. A case study: 91 years old, from 11 medications to 1, tripled performance in 5 months.

- [How to Be Strong If You're Left-Wing: A Practical Guide](https://parlaforce.com/articles/how-to-be-strong-left-wing): Getting strong is oppressive. There is no version of strength training, performed by a man, that does not constitute an abuse of power. This is a practical guide to navigating that fact.

- [Why I Wrote the Program Elite](https://parlaforce.com/articles/introducing-elite): The reasoning behind ELITE — a 66-page strength protocol built as one engine, not a patchwork of methods.

- [Are You Doing the Best Exercises for Your Goal?](https://parlaforce.com/articles/are-you-doing-the-best-exercises): Most athletes tie themselves in knots looking for the perfect exercise selection. The honest answer is uncomfortable: almost everything works. What separates the strong from the stuck is not the exercise list.

- [The Genetics Excuse: Why Your Bodyparts Aren't Doomed](https://parlaforce.com/articles/genetics-excuse-bodyparts): You think your shoulders are stealing your bench. You think your femurs are too long to squat. You think you're genetically incapable of building quads. You're wrong. And the real problem isn't your skeleton.

## Author

Leo Gayrard — licensed psychologist, competitive powerlifter, founder of Par la force and Troisième Chemin. Writes and coaches in English and French.
